author: @randytravis5893
text: I was wondering, you hint that using a thinner coat of graphene, seems like a better result, but what would happen if you made a thick coat of graphene? It seems like the paper seperator you coat the graphene on is what is taking up most of the mass of the super capacitor. Would you get more power storage with a thicker coat of graphene? Also, is there a good liquid separator material that can be thin coated on to your capacitor?  I don't see why you need to have lots of paper, or other solid material, if you can just spray on ultra thin coats of graphene and seperators, I figure you would have more room for more layers if you could just spray the layers on.I was also very interested in your ultrasand. You say silicon dioxide converts carbon into a coating of graphene, under heat. Is that ultrasand a good material for very large scale supercapacitors? The concrete foundation of a house could easly be converted into a huge power storage device with several thin layers of ultrasand grout, with separator layers of laytex paint. Imagine 20 layers of your ultrasand covering the foundation of an entire house, or incorporated into rubber mats put under the flooring of a house. This type of super capacitor would be entirely out of the way, its super cheap and ultra sand seems like a very easy way to mass produce graphene. Also I bet if you made your ultra sand super fine, like flower or powdered sugar, and made a fine grit sandpaper out of it, it would make a wonderful super capacitor.I just love the idea how silicon dioxide is the perfect catalyst, to convert carbon into graphene, it just strikes me why no one has tried to use silicon dioxide to mass produce graphene. I could see, using large rollers of silicon dioxide, and spraying them with a carbon rich spray, then rotating that into a kiln, or a conveyer belt covered with silicon dioxide baking trays, going through an oven, like a bakery makes masses of cookies. Once the silicon dioxide plate exits the oven, the graphene can be scraped off, or chemically washed off.I am looking into making a kiln or a diy brick pizza oven, and try making some of your ultra sand. Could you please tell me if ultra sand would work well for making super capacitors?ThanksRandy

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.text:
Yea, I will give it a try, I have a friend who makes pottery, I think I will ask him to throw the sand into his kiln for the last part of the process.
Thanks so much for all the interesting videos. I am also going to try and make some few layer graphene through electrolysis from alkaline batteries and some pencils, then hit it with some acids, then the hydrogen peroxide. I can get a cheap ultra sound from cdr king.

author: @stephendail4331
text: Hi Robert, I have been reviewing your prolific video productions for a few weeks now, and my head has become a bit supersaturated with all of the concepts and processes you are involved in.  Maybe that state inside my head has congealed a couple of concepts of my own as a result.  Such as a merger of your novel new solar cell device coupled with your supercapacitor into a single unit. Seeing how you used I think it was zinc oxide in your solar cell as the photo conductive agent had me thinking about the more traditional use of silver instead of zinc.  My first profession many  years ago being a photo lab tech, but my memory of those days were a bit foggy, until I also saw your demonstration of using bromine as an intercalating agent to produce graphine in the most simplified manner I've seen to date on that subject. Which process jogged my aging memory to recall the actual active substance in photographic films and prints being silver bromide crystals. Hmm,,,

author: @distantlands862
text: Great videos!  However, can you clarify your calculations?  2000Ah @ 1V is 2000 Wh; that's 7.2 MJ.  Given 0.5CV^2, and allowing for a full discharge, requires a 14.4 megaFarad capacitor.  At your specified 4 F/g, that's 3.6 megaGrams or 3600 kg.  There is no known earthly material with a density of 3600 kg in a volume similar to that ream of paper, which would require about 1175 g/cm^3.  Even osmium is about 22.6 g/cm^3.  The mass density doesn't appear to work out, at least with the above information; perhaps you can comment on this.

replies.author: @ACTSRevolution
replies.text: +Distant Lands   I'm not getting good figures either.  RMS quotes 1.82J (same as Coulombs?) for foil caps, although P=CV or 1/2 (2.7)=1.35.  Anyway, did he say Ah?  I heard Joules = Watts @1sec full discharge.  Over 3600sec this is an energy delivery rate (power) of .55 Watts.  If Robert said the supercap gives 2000 amps at 1volt per second, he would need a visit from police unless he spec'd a maximum deliver duration of 1 second to exhaustion.  Some, I can figure out-- but I have to have good numbers to start.

replies.author: @ACTSRevolution
replies.text: +ACTSRevolution He had been stating 9F/g (he meant 900F/g) and then switched to 2000 Ah!   Why?  These misleads wreck the power of the demonstration -- leaves devils in the details.  If he meant 2000J or 2000Coulombs/Farads, then what do we have?

replies.author: @ACTSRevolution
replies.text:
To add to the curiosity, he has said a 1MF cap would be a good chunk of
the size of a ream, while 900F/g is approx 1MF/kg.  Anyone help?

replies.author: @distantlands862
replies.text: +ACTSRevolution He is certainly a bit "loose" with his terminology sometimes.  For instance, occasionally he uses "power" when he means "energy."  In brief, power (watts) = energy(joules)/time(seconds).  The charge on a capacitor, in coulombs, is Q=CV.  The energy, in joules, is U=0.5CV^2 or U=0.5QV.  The problem with Murray-Smith's calculations here is that the numbers he quotes simply do not allow the requisite capacitor in anything near the small volume he claims.  He has yet to comment on this, but my anticipation is that he either made an orders-of-magnitude calculation error or did a surface area calculation and failed to consider material densities, or some similar error...

replies.author: @distantlands862
replies.text: +ACTSRevolution Well, he said 4 F/g is what he has demonstrated.  The higher values (100's F/gram) were for "active material" only, at least as I gather from the video.  2000 Ah @ 1V is a measure of total energy storage (that is, 2000 Wh or 7.2 MJ), and that's fine for terminology.  However, that's also problem for energy density, since 4 F/g requires a 3600 kg capacitor to meet his 2000 Ah @ 1V.  That is, the 3.6 tonnes of material does not fit into a volume equal to a ream of paper, at least with known earthly materials...

author: @WileHeCoyote
text:
Good Sir Robert Smith,

 Im currently "Binge Watching" all of your videos.

I love the cut of your jib mate! Ive been experimenting with nearly the same set up for a while! (minus your awesome capacitance machine, and electrolyte.)

 I've nearly made a capacitor a thousand times and didn't even think about it lol  

I never even bothered to apply electricity, because i've been researching how to generate it. lol Irony!!

 Ive been researching thermo-electrics and more specifically, thermonic properties of different elements that i paint on or dip coat... ...on to other elements or materials, in my quest to turn fire into electricity. 

 For example i have dipped Copper plates into a copper oxcide + water slurry, let dry, stack, and repeat as needed. Its a decently thermo-electric system, especially if all you can pull together is scrap copper and fire, if you got those, you can also have electricity, and thats a beautiful thing. 

Your process is eerily similar, so ill study your methods, do you proud, and make a 2000ah battery, that can be charged from sitting by a campfire!  

.....correction, i just learned the bit about how graphene turns back to graphite with heat lol, so a thermoelectric graphene battery in one pakage isn't looking  promissing, unless your aware of a chemical process to make graphene thermally stable. lol 

Until then, two "separate" systems, one battery, the other generator and two wires to connect, will do just fine too

author: @donation1913
text:
Have you seen any of the devices created by Donald L. Smith?
Don Smith reverse engineered Tesla's transformer designs and Tesla statements about possibilities with transformer designs. I'm into coil design right now so I find it fascinating. Don smith makes a statement about capacitors about 5 minutes in, here:
https://youtu.be/-NbmheIGG_Y?t=299

Statement: This unit is a 50k watt capacitor. 1 Farad is equal to 1K watt
He's old and his mind is a little worn down from strokes, his math may be off some.

Is there any truth to his statements about capacitors potential energy?

P.S. I figured out how to apply Faradays Law of inductance from here:
http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/electric/farlaw.html#c1

What is not discussed in the formula is how to determine Teslas/s, the formula is pretty straight forward for a moving magnet:
N x A x T/s = Volts
A magnets Gauss / 10000 = Teslas

Conceptually I thought the result was divided by time, it isn't. It is Teslas per second, after some time thinking about it I was able to create a formula which gives you Teslas per second.
(RPM divided by 60) x Number of magnets x (Surface Gauss divided by 2 then convert to Teslas) = Teslas per second

W00t!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Don Ation hiya mate - I do know of Donald - his maths is a bit off a farad is a coulomb per volt and a coulomb is an amp second. A Tesla is a pretty big number mate, Gauss is tiny 1T=10,000 Gauss. So, I think you might have that the wrong way round. Could be worth checking

replies.author: @donation1913
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Don states which laws he uses to arrive at his figures, I'll have to review and post at a later time.

All of Dons devices appear to have dual ground connections, exception being the airplane capacitor. With the output connected to ground there may be a discharge from the earth into the device; The device creating a similar condition in the atmosphere as the cloud discharging lightning, allowing the ground charge to move over a path toward the disturbance, like lightning travels from ground towards the sky meeting the sky discharging toward the ground. In that sense then, it would be a capacitive discharge collector, where our planet is the capacitor.

Gauss divided by ten-thousand equals Teslas is correct.
A magnet rated at brmax 14,800 Gauss
14,800 / 10000 = 1.48 Teslas

The Faraday formula doesn't relate to the capacitor discussion, I had an ahhaaa moment so I thought I'd share. ;-)

Note: brmax is what's available in the center of the magnet, Surface Gauss will be less, pull force can give an idea of SG. From the surface of the magnet into space, there is an exponential drop of Gauss, at 1/8th inch the drop in Gauss is approximately half, if the magnet has 6000 Surface Gauss a coil will receive approximately 3000 Effective Gauss. This is my current understanding.

author: @agentrfr1
text:
Hi +Robert Murray-Smith, that's pretty awesome. Quick question, on your 2000Ah equivalent supercap, what is the charge voltage and farad capacity? I'm trying to get an idea of how many Joules/kWh you have in an EESD the size of a ream of paper :)

Love the videos, cant wait for your interview with Zero!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Agentrfr 1.2v and 9 farads per square cm an a4 is 297 by 210mm and there are 500 double sided cells in a ream - so there are 5613F per side or 5.6Mega Farads per ream thats 6.72 Mega coulombs or 4.06 MegaJoules - it's 3600 coulombs to an amp hour - so it is 1,867 amp hours

replies.author: @agentrfr1
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith cool, so is that confirmation that your EESDs operate at 1.2V? That means that each of your devices get just over 1kWh per "ream of paper" - which is pretty impressive. Love the videos!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Agentrfr I have a range of devices mate - some operate at 2.15 volts some at 1.2 volts some have battery like behaviour some have capacitive like behaviour - we were looking at home made capacitors here

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Hey Robert are you doing any beta testing? We are working on a demo test unit, this uses an AC generator but it is coupled into a 12 kW charge inverter. I would be interested in a beta unit to test with when you get to that point. We will be implementing wind and solar with this as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +VulcanGasifier we are beta testing mate - we have 3 sites and we start that in 4 weeks

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: Nice, Ok late spring we should have funding to support our own system here. I would be interested in a beta unit if available. Ill get with you as we get closer to that point.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +VulcanGasifier ok mate

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Quick question. do you know of any type of dry electrolytes that could work. Specifically in a very high temperature environment. Could an Aerogel possibly work?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +VulcanGasifier NASICON and LISICON are pretty good but expensive

author: @zeropointfuel
text: great videos I was just wondering how much your big test capacitor weighs, I bet that electric car is going to set a few records well done.

replies.text: +zeropointfuel about 500 grams mate

author: @raywharton9425
text: 2000 amp hours would be very impressive. Do I understand correctly that as the Super Capacitor discharges its voltage drops? If these figures hold up the energy density would still be impressive, but we would need some kit to keep the voltages with in the operative ranges of the motors using them.

replies.author: @raywharton9425
replies.text:
+Ray Wharton Maybe put enough in serial to have the voltage well high of what's needed for the application, and have a series of resisters to hold the voltage down that get jumped as the power of the capacitor starts to drain. Then again a very large fraction of the total energy in the cap would be in the relatively high voltage part of is discharge curve.

Seriously, bicycle!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ray Wharton very true mate

replies.author: @dft9786
replies.text: +Ray Wharton By the time voltage drops to 60% it will be 2/3 energy depleted or something like that. Maybe by the time it is 20-30% there will be so little left it wont be worth to recover it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +dft what you are saying is true of all capacitors mate

replies.author: @dft9786
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith that is why there is no reason to try and  recover it all the way down to zero voltage. resonable range will suffice

author: @proteinman1981
text:
I really enjoyed these capacitor videos, thank you very much for sharing your knowledge and experience. 

When making a huge capacitor won't the weight of the current collectors come into play? Or is there a way to have the active material as the collector.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +proteinman1981 there is a way to have just the active material as the collector - but it is a bit more complicated and i wanted this as a do it yourself home tutorial

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
Rob: "2000 Amp Hours"
Me: "bleedin' 'ek I wonder how long it lasts though"
Rob: "After 10,000 hours it has lost some capacity"
Me: "right, that's loads, let me grab my calculator....."
Rob "It's down to 97% now..."
Me: *puts calculator away

author: @ZeroPointAlpha
text: Definitely interested in the implications for mobile robotics at this point. To put it in perspective, Asimo (regarded to be one of the world's most advanced humanoid robots) has a run-time of about 1 hour, and takes THREE HOURS to charge, and the battery weighs about 13 pounds (6kg). If these capacitors can provide similar power characteristics with shorter charge times and less weight, the possibilities are endless.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +ZeroPointAlpha it will certainly help a bit - wait until i reveal the next one - it's 4 amp hour per gram - asimo will have 24,000 Amps at 2.5 volts lol

replies.author: @ZeroPointAlpha
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
His battery actually measures 51.8V, so you'd have to stack a fair amount in series. :V

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +ZeroPointAlpha his battery might do mate - but what does a cell measure - how big is it and what energy density does it have?

replies.author: @ZeroPointAlpha
replies.text: Sadly, all I know is what they've released on the FAQ, which I've already provided (besides that it's Li-Ion, but that's a given in most devices these days where weight and energy density are a concern). All I'm saying is that about 26 of your capacitors in a series/parallel arrangement would potentially be a suitable replacement, particularly if the weight is also reduced.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +ZeroPointAlpha could be mate
